---
title: "What did we actually measure? Sensitivity vs. a shift in means"
---

This chapter addresses one of the most consequential decisions in *both* simulation
studies — one that is easy to miss precisely because the framing is so intuitive. The
mean-shift findings are real and publishable; what needs sharpening is what they
are actually *measuring*. The key definitions are on pp. 48, 62, and 65, the same rule is
repeated at p. 86, and the results are in Tables 23–27 (pp. 110–112).

## What the dissertation does

The dissertation defines sensitivity exactly as it should be defined. Page 48 states that "the
sensitivity of rater effect indicators refers to the ability of these indices to
**accurately identify rater effects when they are present**" — that is, given that a
rater truly has a problem, does the index catch it? This is the *detection* question, and
it is the right one.

To measure it, both studies run an ANOVA over the simulated conditions and report partial
η² as the outcome: "we examined partial η² as an effect size, considering values greater
than 0.01 as the threshold for practical significance" (p. 62; the same rule in Study 3,
p. 86). The analysis is restricted to effect raters — "we focused only on the effect
raters" (p. 65) — and the resulting η² values are read as evidence of sensitivity (for
example, rater location η² = .51 and HRM ψ η² = .88; Tables 23–27, pp. 110–112).

The logic appears natural: if the index rises when there is a real effect, and the effect of
condition on the index is large, the index would seem to be detecting the effect. But the step
from "the mean shifts" to "individual raters are caught" is exactly the step that needs
unpacking — and unpacking it changes the picture.

## Why this threatens the conclusion

The gap is clearest by analogy. Consider a school nurse with a
thermometer, asked to find which children have a fever. *Detection* asks two questions: of
the children who truly have a fever, what fraction does the thermometer flag (the
**true-positive rate**, TPR)? And of the healthy children, what fraction does it flag by
mistake (the **false-positive rate**, FPR)? The **ROC curve** traces the trade-off between
those two across every possible cut score, and the **area under it (AUC)** summarizes the
whole thing in one number — 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is a coin flip. (Geometrically: plot TPR
against FPR as the cut slides from strict to lax; a perfect detector hugs the top-left
corner, AUC = 1, while a coin flip lies along the diagonal, AUC = 0.5.)

Partial η² answers a *different* question. It quantifies how much of the variance in
*thermometer readings* is explained by whether the child is sick. A thermometer can show a
large group-mean gap — sick kids average 39°C, healthy kids 37°C — while still flagging
one in six healthy children, because normal temperatures vary and the cut falls inside the
normal range. The big η² says the thermometer is sensitive to sickness *as a
population-level signal*; it does not say the thermometer is accurate at the
*individual-level decision*.

This is not hypothetical. The measurement model was reconstructed in R from the simulation
parameters described in the dissertation — not a re-run of TAM, but a ground-up implementation. In a
severity scenario at the field-standard **+0.5-logit cut**, the reconstruction recovers η² ≈ 0.80 and
AUC ≈ 0.997 — both excellent. But the false-positive rate at that cut is about **16%**,
because normal-rater severities are drawn from N(0, 0.5) and +0.5 falls exactly one
standard deviation above the mean, so by construction about one in six normal raters clears
it. That 16% is never visible in the dissertation, because
analyzing *effect raters only* makes the false-positive side of the problem structurally
absent from the data. @fig-sensitivity makes this concrete.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sensitivity
#| fig-cap: "η² is not detection. **(A)** Even with a huge η² (0.80) and an excellent AUC (0.997), the field-standard +0.5-logit cut flags ≈ 16% of *normal* raters as severe (shaded tail) — a false-positive rate the effect-raters-only analysis never reports. **(B)** Across conditions, the study's η² ≥ .01 'practical significance' bar lands at AUC ≈ 0.56 — barely above the 0.5 chance line. A large mean shift and good individual detection are different things."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4
#| code-summary: "Show the R code (severity detection + η²→AUC map)"
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork); library(scales) })
source("R/common.R")
set.seed(303)

# model-information weight W = average score variance; SE of a rater location
d_vals <- rnorm(5, 0, 0.5); thL <- rnorm(40000)
W  <- mean(vapply(d_vals, function(d) mean(score_variance(pcmfrm_probs(thL, 0, d, alpha = 1))), numeric(1)))
se_lambda <- function(N, I = 5) 1 / sqrt(N * I * W)

# ---- Panel A: severity detection at N=100, cut = +0.5 -----------------------
SE <- se_lambda(100); CUT <- 0.5; NR <- 8000
lam_norm <- rnorm(NR, 0, 0.5) + rnorm(NR, 0, SE)
lam_sev  <- rnorm(NR, 2, 0.5) + rnorm(NR, 0, SE)
eta2_two <- function(a, b) { g <- c(a, b); gm <- mean(g)
  (length(a)*(mean(a)-gm)^2 + length(b)*(mean(b)-gm)^2) / sum((g-gm)^2) }
eta2_A <- eta2_two(lam_norm, lam_sev)
TPR_A <- mean(lam_sev > CUT); FPR_A <- mean(lam_norm > CUT); AUC_A <- mean(outer(lam_sev, lam_norm, ">"))

xs <- seq(-1.5, 5.5, length.out = 600); sdmix <- sqrt(0.5^2 + SE^2)
dens <- rbind(data.frame(x = xs, y = dnorm(xs, 0, sdmix), g = "Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))"),
              data.frame(x = xs, y = dnorm(xs, 2, sdmix), g = "Severe raters (~N(2, 0.5))"))
dens$g <- factor(dens$g, levels = c("Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))", "Severe raters (~N(2, 0.5))"))
fp <- subset(dens, g == "Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))" & x >= CUT)
box <- sprintf("η² = %.2f   (huge effect)\nAUC = %.3f\nTPR = %.3f\nFPR = %.3f  ← not reported here\n          (effect raters only)",
               eta2_A, AUC_A, TPR_A, FPR_A)
pA <- ggplot(dens, aes(x, y, colour = g)) +
  geom_area(data = fp, aes(x, y), fill = pal_companion[["none"]], alpha = .4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = CUT, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey30", linewidth = .7) +
  annotate("text", x = CUT + .08, y = max(dens$y) * .85, label = "+0.5-logit\ncut", hjust = 0,
           size = 3, colour = "grey30", lineheight = .85) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.55, y = .10, label = "false\npositives", size = 3, colour = pal_companion[["none"]],
           fontface = "bold", lineheight = .9) +
  annotate("label", x = 5.4, y = max(dens$y) * .62, label = box, hjust = 1, vjust = 1,
           size = 2.85, lineheight = 1.1, fill = "white", colour = "grey20") +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Normal raters (~N(0, 0.5))" = pal_companion[["none"]],
                                              "Severe raters (~N(2, 0.5))" = pal_companion[["severe"]])) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 5, 1)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  A huge η² hides a 16% false-positive rate",
       subtitle = "estimated rater location (logits); N = 100 persons per rater",
       x = "estimated rater severity  λ̂  (logits)", y = "density") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom",
                              plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9))

# ---- Panel B: η² -> detection map -------------------------------------------
set.seed(303)
dfB <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq(0.05, 3, length.out = 40), function(mu) {
  a <- rnorm(6000, 0, .5); b <- rnorm(6000, mu, .5)
  data.frame(eta2 = eta2_two(a, b), auc = mean(outer(b, a, ">")), tpr = mean(b > CUT)) }))
i01 <- which.min(abs(dfB$eta2 - 0.01)); auc01 <- dfB$auc[i01]
eta2_seq <- seq(1e-4, .92, length.out = 400); d_seq <- 2 * sqrt(eta2_seq / (1 - eta2_seq))
ana <- subset(data.frame(eta2 = eta2_seq, auc = pnorm(d_seq / sqrt(2))), auc >= .45)
pB <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = ana, aes(eta2, auc), colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], linewidth = .6, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_line(data = dfB, aes(eta2, auc, colour = "ROC-AUC"), linewidth = .9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = .5, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey55", linewidth = .65) +
  annotate("text", x = .0011, y = .505, label = "AUC = 0.5  (chance)", hjust = 0, vjust = 0, size = 2.9, colour = "grey45") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = .01, linetype = "dashed", colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], linewidth = .75) +
  annotate("text", x = .013, y = .63, hjust = 0, vjust = .5, size = 2.85, lineheight = .9,
           colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], fontface = "italic",
           label = sprintf("η² = .01\n\"practical\nsignificance\"\n→ AUC ≈ %.2f\n(near chance)", auc01)) +
  geom_point(data = dfB[i01, ], aes(eta2, auc), colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(.001, .01, .05, .1, .3, .6, .9), labels = c(".001", ".01", ".05", ".10", ".30", ".60", ".90")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(.45, 1.01), breaks = seq(.5, 1, .1), oob = scales::squish) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("ROC-AUC" = pal_companion[["severe"]])) +
  labs(title = "(B)  η² ≥ .01 ≠ ability to detect a rater",
       subtitle = "two groups N(0, 0.5) vs N(μ, 0.5); dotted = analytic AUC = Φ(d/√2)",
       x = "partial η²  (log scale)", y = "detection metric") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom",
                              plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
pA | pB
```

The η² ≥ .01 threshold compounds the problem. A partial η² of .01 corresponds to Cohen's
$d \approx 0.20$, which corresponds to **AUC ≈ 0.557** — barely distinguishable from chance
(Panel B). The bar says "practically significant," but it is set so low that an index can
clear it while doing almost no useful individual-level discrimination. The figure does not
indict the findings; it shows that η² and AUC track *different things*, and that
a single bar should not be asked to certify both.

## What would fix it

::: {.callout-tip}
## Fix-it
The revision is more *additive* than corrective — the mean-shift results stay; they just
need company.

- **Report detection directly — the full confusion-matrix readout.** For each indicator and
  condition, give, at each stated cut, the true-positive rate (sensitivity — fraction of
  effect raters correctly flagged), the false-positive rate (fraction of normal raters
  wrongly flagged) and its complement specificity (1 − FPR), plus the predictive values that
  tell a *reader* what a flag is worth — PPV (of the raters flagged, the fraction that truly
  have an effect) and NPV (of those cleared, the fraction truly normal). Add the
  threshold-free summary, the ROC curve with its AUC, and a **calibration** check: does the
  realized flag rate among normal raters match the nominal rate the cut is meant to deliver
  (a +0.5-logit cut on $N(0, 0.5)$ should flag ≈ 16%, not whatever an off-target cut
  produces)? And because TPR, FPR, and AUC are themselves averages over the 100 replications,
  report a **Monte-Carlo standard error** for each, so a reader can tell a genuine difference
  between indicators from simulation noise.
- **Pre-specify a direction-aware flag rule, then report detection at it.** Severity has a
  sign: a one-sided +0.5-logit cut catches only *over-severity* and is structurally blind to
  leniency (the negative tail), so a too-lenient rater is missed no matter how large the
  effect. Decide the rule *before* looking at the results — pre-register a two-sided
  severity/leniency flag ($|\hat{\lambda}| > c$), or a pair of explicit one-sided cuts ($+c$
  for severity, $-c$ for leniency, reported separately) — and report TPR/FPR/AUC at that
  pre-registered rule rather than at a cut chosen after seeing which side the effect fell on.
- **Include both normal and effect raters.** The false-positive rate is undefined if
  normal raters are excluded — so the analysis must contain them.
- **Add parameter recovery** already latent in the design: bias, RMSE, and coverage of
  the rater-effect estimates — *how well* the index estimates, alongside *whether* it flags.
- **Justify or replace the η² ≥ .01 bar.** If kept, say plainly that it marks a non-trivial
  *mean shift*, not acceptable *detection accuracy*; better, report AUC next to η².
- **Reserve "sensitivity" for the detection quantity** (TPR / ROC / AUC), and describe the
  η² results as "how much the mean index shifts across conditions" — a real, reportable
  finding, but a different claim.
:::

## What a reviewer will ask

::: {.callout-warning}
## What a reviewer will ask
"Your definition of sensitivity is detection — correctly identifying problem raters. Why
report the partial η² of the mean index on effect raters only, rather than the
true-positive and false-positive rates and the ROC-AUC at your cut scores? And what
justifies an η² ≥ .01 'practical significance' bar that corresponds to near-chance
detection?"
:::

A prepared answer is available: the η² analysis was a condition-sensitivity check
— showing the index responds to an effect at all — but *individual-level*
detection requires TPR, FPR, and AUC, and the revised manuscript can report them directly.
That response frames the revision as a strengthening, not
a correction. This chapter unlocks the others: once "sensitivity" means
detection and is measured as detection, the earlier concerns about ψ, the misfit
manipulation, and the MCMC all become easier to state precisely and to answer.
